ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKM covers composite meat dishes; used as closest defensible anchor for this mutton curry and roti set. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Roganjosh specifically.

main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, bay leaf, cinnamon)

serving.description: 1 set (mutton roganjosh portion with 2 tandoori roti)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 380 g, comprising a portion of mutton roganjosh curry (approximately 250 g) and two tandoori roti (approximately 65 g each), as commonly served in Singapore Indian restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Roganjosh And Roti Set — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database for this composite dish (cr_id G00010476). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from the HPB record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is not separately reported by HPB for this record; estimated from ICMR-NIN data for mutton curry and tandoori roti components. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several vitamins are not reported by HPB for this dish and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on wheat roti / chapati (GI ~62) as the dominant carbohydrate contributor in this set meal
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mutton Roganjosh and Roti Set as a composite dish. The GI of 62 is estimated using wheat roti / chapati as the primary carbohydrate vehicle (GI ~62, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021). The mutton curry component contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (62 × 28.3) / 100 ≈ 17.5; however, accounting for the mixed-meal effect and protein/fat attenuation, a conservative GL of 11.0 per 380 g serving is applied. Serving carbohydrate: 7.44 g/100g × 380 g / 100 = 28.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 467 mg/100 g; estimated 1775 mg per 380 g serving, exceeding 77% of the 2300 mg daily limit in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium content; if consumed, request reduced-salt preparation and avoid adding condiments or pickles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Roti component contributes significant carbohydrate load (~28 g per serving); medium GI of 62 from wheat roti raises postprandial glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit roti to one piece, choose whole wheat roti over maida, and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (467 mg/100 g), elevated saturated fat (~4.2 g/100 g from mutton and ghee), and cholesterol (85 mg/100 g) collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally, request roti without ghee, and choose a smaller mutton portion to reduce saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton is high in arachidonic acid (pro-inflammatory omega-6); Kashmiri chili is a nightshade; sodium at 467 mg/100 g worsens tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to the combination of red meat arachidonic acid load, nightshade ingredients, and high sodium; substituting mutton with chicken or legumes and omitting ghee from roti would reduce inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mutton Roganjosh and Roti Set is a popular Indian restaurant set meal served widely in Singapore, consisting of a portion of mutton roganjosh curry accompanied by two tandoori roti. Roganjosh is a slow-braised Kashmiri mutton curry characterised by its deep red colour derived from Kashmiri dried red chili and its aromatic spice profile of cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, fennel, and ginger. The mutton is cooked until tender in a yogurt-and-ghee-based gravy, yielding a rich, mildly spiced, slightly tangy sauce with a thick, glossy consistency. Tandoori roti is an unleavened flatbread baked in a tandoor oven, producing a slightly charred, chewy bread used to scoop the curry. Per 100 g, the set provides 192 kcal, 14.05 g protein, 11.58 g fat, and 7.44 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 467 mg and cholesterol at 85 mg. The high protein content reflects the mutton component, while the moderate fat content comes from both the meat and ghee used in preparation. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium (estimated 1775 mg per 380 g serving), making it a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The roti component raises the glycemic load to a medium level (GI ~62), requiring portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal-suitable and is commonly consumed at lunch or dinner in Indian restaurants across Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010476) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from ICMR-NIN component data. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (wheat roti/chapati). All mandatory multilingual names provided. Ontology anchors are defensible approximations with documented gap notes.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.2 g/100 g) is estimated from ICMR-NIN data for mutton curry and tandoori roti components, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this composite dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin panel, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. 3. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on wheat roti/chapati from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021); no direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal. 4. Food group has been reclassified from 'Grains and staples / Breads and flatbreads' to 'Meat and alternatives / Curries and stews with bread' to better reflect the dominant protein-and-fat nutritional profile of the composite dish. 5. Serving size of 380 g is an estimate based on typical Singapore restaurant portioning (250 g curry + 2 × 65 g roti); no standardised portion weight is recorded in the HPB source.
